lede: Dealers and wholesale buyers don't pay retail. A price list is a stack of RULES the pricing engine walks at add-to-cart time — cost-plus markup, %-off-list tiers, volume brackets — resolved deterministically by scope and quantity. This guide builds the classic dealer rule: Cost Price + 30%, the same 'landed cost plus margin' every distributor uses, with the account's own cost as the base so the margin holds even as purchase prices move.

steps.title: Open Price Lists
steps.narration: A deployment can run many price lists — retail, a channel-specific list, one per dealer tier. Each is a named container of rules with a currency and an active flag. Products are shared company-wide; the price a given buyer sees is whichever list resolves for them, so the list is the pricing dimension, not the product.

steps.title: Create the dealer price list
steps.narration: Name it for the segment it serves — 'Dealer / Wholesale — Cost Plus'. Currency is the tenant default. Leave Channel unset to apply it across every sales surface, or scope it to one channel (POS, a specific storefront) when only that surface should see dealer prices. The list is empty until you add rules.

steps.title: Add a Cost-Plus rule
steps.narration: Set Computation to Formula and Base to Cost Price — this is what makes it cost-PLUS: the rule builds the price up from what YOU paid, not down from a retail sticker. Base 'Cost Price' reads the product's own cost, so the dealer margin holds automatically when purchase prices change. 'Markup % over base' is the headline field: enter 30 and the engine computes cost × 1.30. Markup applies first (builds the price); an optional Discount % then trims it, a Surcharge adds a flat handling fee, and Round-to-nearest tidies the result.

steps.title: How the engine resolves a dealer price
steps.narration: At add-to-cart the pricing engine finds every rule that applies (by scope and quantity), sorts them — scope rank, then higher min-quantity, then priority ascending (LOWER priority runs first) — and applies the winner. For a product costing 10,000 (minor units), this rule returns 13,000: cost × (1 + 30/100). Add a second rule with a higher Min Qty and its own markup to reward volume; the bracket with the highest satisfied Min Qty wins. The whole resolution is in-process — pricing never makes an HTTP hop on the hot path.
